title: Chapter 8: The First Rule
content: The door didn't open.

Jane turned the handle again — a smooth, practiced motion she'd performed dozens of times over the past weeks. The latch didn't catch. The heavy oak panel didn't swing toward her. She pushed, felt the frame resist, and the absence of give registered in her body before her mind caught up: the door was locked.

She had not locked it. She'd been working with it propped ajar, a rubber wedge from the conservation kit holding it open to circulate air through the archive's stale, paper-thick atmosphere. She'd propped it herself. She'd checked it twice.
Jane pressed her palm flat against the wood and pushed harder. The door didn't budge. The iron hinges were older than the Third Republic; they would hold against far more than her shoulder.
Then, from the corridor outside, a voice. Flat. Uninflected. The vocal equivalent of a locked door.
"Mr. Rochester prefers the archive accessed during staffed hours only, Miss Eyre."
Grace Poole.

A click — the second turn of a key, consolidating the lock — and then footsteps, measured and unhurried, retreating down the stone corridor. Jane listened until the sound dissolved into the ambient hum of the château's heating system, until there was nothing left but the silence of a room with one exit and no windows.
She did not panic.

This was not courage. This was calculation, cold and instant, the same faculty that had carried her through Lowood's institutional malice and the interminable months of freelance conservation work that paid in exposure and dried lentils. Fear was information. Showing fear in this house was handing information to whoever was watching, and someone was always watching in Thornfield. The cameras in the corridors. Grace Poole's patrols. The odd, tinny security panel in the kitchen that clicked and hummed whenever a door opened.
Jane pressed her ear to the keyhole. Nothing. Grace was gone — or waiting far enough away that her silence was indistinguishable from absence. Either way, the door was locked, and Jane was alone.

She straightened her coat and called through the wood, keeping her voice light, conversational, as if being locked in a room were a minor inconvenience rather than a controlled demonstration of power.
"Mrs. Poole? I seem to have been working past hours. Could you let me out?"
A pause. Then Grace's voice, milder now, almost pleasant: "I'll inform Mr. Rochester you're still in the archive. He'll be pleased you're so dedicated."
So dedicated.

Three words, silk-wrapped steel. Grace was not merely locking her in — she was reporting her. Everything in this house was reported: where you walked, what you touched, how long you stayed. The archive register, the corridor cameras, the housekeeper's unblinking surveillance. And now this — Jane's presence, after hours, in a room full of garments she had no authorization to examine. Grace Poole was filing a report in Edward Rochester's mental ledger, and the entry would read: *inquisitive, unmonitored, potentially compromised*.
Jane stepped back from the door. Her pulse had settled. Good.
She surveyed the archive with new eyes.

The long room stretched before her in the amber wash of conservation lighting — low-wattage, UV-filtered, designed to keep the textiles from degrading. The garment racks stood in soldierly rows, their protective covers glowing like shrouds. The scent of cedar and acid-free tissue filled the air, underlaid with something older — the faint, animal musk of silk that had been worn, sweated in, lived in. Along the far wall, the biodegradable silk collection hung in its designated climate-controlled bay, the pieces she'd been examining when the door was locked.
If she was going to be trapped here, she was going to use the time.

Jane crossed to the HELP-code garments with deliberate purpose. She'd already catalogued the stitching patterns: the H-E-L-P sequence, worked into seam allowances and hemlines with red thread, invisible unless you knew where to look — and cared to look, which no one at Thornfield apparently had for five years. She pulled the rack out and knelt, scanning the lowest row where the oldest pieces were stored, the ones from the earliest production runs, the ones no curator or buyer would request because their fabric was already yellowing at the edges.

There. A camisole, 2019 production tag, biodegradable silk so early-stage it was practically prototype. The HELP code ran along the interior bodice seam, five stitches of red thread barely visible against the off-white silk. Jane examined the other garments on the same rack. This one was the least likely to be inspected for any reason — too degraded for display, too early for retrospectives, tucked in a corner where light barely reached.

She took a pair of fine-point tweezers from the conservation kit and bent close. Her hands were steady. She'd removed degraded fibers from Coptic textiles older than the cathedral at Chartres; she could extract a single thread segment without leaving a trace. She gripped the red fiber at its midpoint and snipped it free with surgical shears, taking a length no longer than her little finger — enough for analysis, too short to create a visible gap in the stitching pattern. The remaining stitches held their shape. No one would notice.

She wrapped the thread segment in a cotton archival sleeve — standard conservation supply, unremarkable, would attract no attention — and slipped it inside the lining of her Thornfield-issued coat, tucking it into the seam where the brand label was stitched. The thread pressed against the fabric, a sliver of evidence no wider than a matchstick, invisible unless someone tore the coat apart.
Evidence. Physical evidence she could carry out of this room, past whatever eyes waited outside.
Jane buttoned the coat and stood.

The archive was silent. The conservation lights hummed their low amber drone. Somewhere in the walls, the heating pipes ticked — the house's circulatory system, pushing warmth through stone veins that had been laid centuries before anyone thought to wire electricity through them.
Electricity.

Jane's gaze tracked along the wall above the garment racks, past the fire suppression panel, past the climate control display, to a fixture she'd noticed on her first day in the archive and promptly dismissed as decorative: a brass intercom horn, mounted on a plate of tarnished brass, with a single toggling switch and a rotary dial numbered one through twelve. It was old — 1940s, she'd guessed, from the same era the château was first wired for modern utilities. The plate was stamped with a maker's mark she half-recognized: a Loire Valley electrical firm that had gone out of business before de Gaulle returned to power.
She'd assumed it was dead. Everything old in Thornfield was either decorative or dysfunctional — the aesthetic of curated decay that Edward Rochester wore like a second skin.

But the housekeeper's station had a working intercom. Jane had heard Grace Poole use it, that first week, paging the kitchen for service. And the kitchen had answered. Which meant the system had circuit continuity — the copper wiring inside these walls still carried signal. The château's thick stone walls killed cellular signals on contact, but they couldn't kill a closed-circuit copper line that ran through the stone itself. That was the irony of Thornfield: the 1940s infrastructure outperformed the twenty-first century because it was analog, hardwired, impervious to the dead zones that swallowed every phone signal within these walls.

Jane pulled a chair beneath the intercom and climbed up. The brass was cold under her fingers — not room temperature, colder, as if the metal retained the chill of decades. She toggled the switch to ON. A faint pop of static. The system was alive.

She turned the rotary dial to extension 7. Adèle's room. Jane had memorized the room assignments during her first week; it was the kind of detail that cost nothing to collect and might, someday, be worth something. Today it was worth everything.
She pressed the call button.
Static. Then a click, and Adèle's voice, groggy and half-asleep: "Hello?"
"Adèle. It's Jane."
"Jane? You're on the — how are you on the intercom? That thing is from the Jurassic period."
"It still works. Adèle, I'm locked in the archive. Could you bring my phone? I left it charging in my room, on the nightstand."
A pause. "Locked in?"
"The door. It won't open."

Another pause. Jane could hear the girl processing — the confusion, the faint alarm, and then the practical override of someone who had grown up in this house and learned that strange things happened here and were not discussed.
"I'm coming," Adèle said.

The line clicked dead. Jane toggled the intercom off and climbed down from the chair. She returned it to its place beneath the conservation desk. She placed the tweezers and shears back in the kit. She arranged herself on the stool nearest the garment rack, hands folded, face composed, the picture of an archivist who had simply worked late and found herself inconvenienced by a stuck lock.
She did not have to wait long.

The door swung open — unlocked, unresisting — and Adèle stood in the corridor with Jane's phone in one hand and a look of puzzled irritation on her face. She was wearing an oversized Thornfield hoodie and slippers shaped like cartoon foxes. Her hair was gathered in a messy topknot.
"The door wasn't locked when I got here," Adèle said.
Jane rose from the stool. "It must have come unstuck," she said evenly. "The lock is old."

"Bien sûr, everything in this place is old." Adèle handed her the phone. "You know there's an intercom? Like, an actual working intercom? I thought those were decorative. Like everything else that's broken here."
"It worked," Jane said. "Thank you, Adèle. For the phone."
Adèle shrugged, already retreating down the corridor. "Sure. Don't stay in there all night, d'accord? It's creepy enough during the day."
Jane watched her go. The girl didn't understand. She would.

Grace Poole was nowhere in the corridor. Jane checked both directions, confirmed the absence, and walked back to her room with measured steps — not too fast, not too slow, nothing that would read as flight on a security camera she couldn't see.
In her room, she locked the door and sat on the edge of the bed.

The phone showed no signal. The Wi-Fi indicator was dark. The cellular bars had vanished the moment she'd arrived at Thornfield, and they had not returned. But the intercom — analog, copper-wired, running through the bones of the house — had connected her to Adèle in seconds. A system from 1948 had outperformed every piece of modern technology she carried. It was almost funny. Almost.
She pulled the cotton archival sleeve from her coat lining and held it under her phone's flashlight. The red thread lay coiled against the white cotton like a vein extracted from silk.

The light was harsh, clinical. Under its white glare, the thread revealed itself: organic fiber, not synthetic — she could see the irregular spin, the slight thickness variation that marked handspun work. The dye was uneven, deeper in some strands, lighter where the color hadn't penetrated fully. Madder root. She'd seen enough natural dyes in her career to recognize the warm, slightly orange undertone that distinguished madder from cochineal or synthetic red. This was not factory-produced thread. It was made — spun by hand, dyed by hand, stitched by hand.

By someone with access to raw materials — silk waste, dye stuffs, the detritus of a textile atelier — but not to tools. No spinning wheel; the thread was too fine and too uneven for wheel-spun work, which meant it had been spun on a hand spindle, or even between fingers. No manufactured dye bath; the madder was uneven because it had been applied in small quantities, something the dyer could heat on a makeshift rig, a pot on a hot plate perhaps, the kind of setup you could hide in a basement.
By someone in a basement. Spinning her own thread to communicate with a world that had forgotten she was there.
Jane's jaw tightened. She turned off the flashlight and sat in the dark.
The phone screen glowed. One notification, downloaded in the brief window before the signal had died for good: a message from Helen Burns.
> Found something. Supply chain doesn't add up. Don't trust anyone there. Reply ASAP.

Jane read it twice. Three times. The words didn't change. *Don't trust anyone there.* Helen — who had never set foot in Thornfield, who had only the supply chain records and her own relentless cross-referencing to go on — was telling Jane what Jane already knew but hadn't let herself articulate: the rot in this house was structural. It wasn't one person. It wasn't one locked door or one intercepted night. It was the architecture itself — every wall a witness, every corridor a conduit for information that flowed one direction, toward the man who owned the keys.

Jane couldn't reply. No signal. No Wi-Fi. The intercom connected rooms inside Thornfield; it did not connect to the outside world. She was sealed in a castle of analog silence, with evidence pressed against her ribs and a message from the only person she trusted that she could not answer.

She went to the window. The grounds spread below in the moonlight — the formal hedges, the gravel paths, the dark mouth of the garden maze where Edward had walked beside her three evenings ago and told her about "honest luxury." *This house doesn't hide anything, Jane. What you see is what exists.* His voice in her memory, warm and sure, the voice of a man who believed his own performance. Or believed it enough that the difference no longer mattered.
Every word was a lie.

She turned from the window and looked at her reflection in the dark glass: a woman in a black coat with a stolen thread hidden in the lining and a phone that couldn't call for help. She had a thread — handspun, hand-dyed, evidence that someone inside Thornfield had been screaming in silk for five years and no one had listened. She had a name — Bertha Mason, the wife who existed in paperwork and nowhere else. She had a file on Edward Rochester's desk that she could not access, behind a door she could not open without alerting the entire house. She had no way out and no one she could trust.
Except.

Adèle. Nineteen years old, naive, dressed in fox slippers, who had come when Jane called because someone asked her for help and it didn't occur to her to refuse. Adèle, who moved through this house like a child walking through a museum of dangerous things she didn't recognize.

And Bertha. A woman Jane had never met, who had been spinning thread in a basement for half a decade, stitching HELP into garments that traveled the world while she could not leave her floor. A woman who had found a way to speak when every other door was closed.
The clock was ticking. Jane had evidence now, but Edward had the keys to every door. She needed to get to the basement. She needed to meet the ghost.
Helen said don't trust anyone. But Jane may have to trust one person.
The question wasn't whether. The question was who.
